text.en: After a party or large gathering, I leave with more energy than when I arrived.
I work out my ideas by talking them through with someone.
In a group discussion, I speak up early without waiting to be asked.
I strike up conversations with strangers — in a queue, on a plane — without needing a special reason.
When something good happens to me, my first impulse is to tell someone right away.
After a long day alone, I look for a reason to call or meet someone.
I start answering a question right away, shaping my thought as I speak.
Given the choice, I work or study in a lively place — a café or shared room — rather than in silence.
At meetings, I volunteer to speak or present.
I plan my weekends around get-togethers with groups of people.
After a few hours in company, I need time alone before I can do anything else.
I rehearse what I am going to say in my head before saying it aloud.
I sometimes let a call go unanswered so I can prepare before calling back.
When facing a difficult problem, I first work it out alone in a quiet place, and only then discuss it.
In conversation, I let others finish and often keep my reaction to myself.
I keep a new idea to myself until it is fully formed.
At big events, I find one person for a quiet conversation at the edge of the room.
I turn down invitations because I would rather spend the evening by myself.
When asked a question, I pause to think before answering.
When assembling something new, I follow the written instructions step by step.
When someone explains an idea, I ask for a concrete example before accepting it.
I remember exact facts from what I read — dates, prices, names.
When describing an event, I recount what actually happened, in order and with specifics.
When buying, I compare exact specifications rather than general impressions.
I prefer tasks with a clear, proven method over tasks that require inventing an approach.
When planning a trip, I focus on the practical details — schedules, costs, distances.
I judge a proposal mainly by whether it has already worked somewhere.
I do my best work on tasks where the requirements are spelled out precisely.
In a book or article, I skip the theory and look for the practical takeaway.
When I learn a new fact, I immediately fit it into a bigger pattern or idea.
I catch myself imagining how things could be, rather than noting how they are.
In conversation, I drift from what is happening now to future possibilities.
A new idea can absorb me more than the practical task in front of me.
I invent my own way of solving a problem even when a standard one exists.
I explain things through images, metaphors, and analogies.
Untested, new ways of doing things attract me more than proven ones.
I notice connections between things that seem unrelated to others.
After a film or book, I think more about its meaning than about the details of the plot.
When deciding, I weigh the pros and cons and follow the conclusion, even if it upsets someone.
In a discussion, I directly point out flaws in the other person's reasoning.
I give honest critical feedback even when I know it will disappoint.
When two people disagree, I judge who is right by the facts, not by who is more upset.
I convince people with evidence and reasoning rather than appeals to feelings.
If a rule is fair, I apply it even to a close friend.
I prefer an uncomfortable truth to a comforting version of events.
In group decisions, I argue for the objectively strongest option even against the group's mood.
I evaluate a person's work on its merits, separately from my relationship with them.
I question the logic of a claim even when it comes from someone I like.
Before deciding, I first think through how each option will affect the people involved.
I soften critical feedback so the person is not hurt.
I go along with a group decision I privately disagree with, to preserve the peace.
When someone shares a problem, I comfort them first and analyze later.
I make exceptions to rules when applying them would hurt someone.
In a debate, I sense the emotional temperature of the room before I weigh the arguments.
I change my plans to fit what matters to the people close to me.
I persuade by connecting to what a person cares about, not by proving them wrong.
Choosing between the "correct" option and the one that keeps harmony, I choose harmony.
I keep a to-do list and actually cross items off.
I finish tasks well before the deadline.
I plan my week in advance and mostly follow the plan.
Before starting work, I put my workspace in order.
I completely finish one task before starting the next.
When traveling, I book tickets and lodging well in advance.
When plans change suddenly, I immediately build a new plan rather than acting ad hoc.
My files, papers, and belongings each have their fixed place.
I keep a fixed daily routine.
Once a decision is made, I close the question and do not reopen it.
I decide what to do with my day as it unfolds.
I work in bursts of inspiration rather than on a schedule.
My best work happens close to the deadline.
I start new projects before finishing old ones.
I keep plans loose so I can change them if something better comes up.
My workspace has piles that make sense only to me.
I put off final decisions to keep my options open longer.
When traveling, I prefer to sort things out on the spot rather than book everything ahead.
I drop a plan midway if a more interesting path appears.
After an unpleasant conversation, I return to my usual state within minutes.
Bad news rarely changes how I spend the rest of the day.
Under time pressure, I keep working at my normal pace.
I can listen to criticism of my work and get back to it within the hour.
My mood stays roughly level through the week, regardless of events.
When something breaks or falls through, I go straight to fixing it without dwelling on it.
Waiting for important results does not affect my sleep.
A sudden change of plans doesn't knock me off track — I adjust and continue.
In a heated argument, my voice and pace stay the same as in a calm conversation.
After a conflict, I replay it in my head for hours or even days.
A small setback can color my whole day.
I feel events in my body — racing heart, tight stomach — before I've fully processed them.
My mood can shift noticeably several times in one day.
Criticism stays with me long after the conversation is over.
Before important events, I sleep worse than usual.
A stray remark can occupy my thoughts for the rest of the evening.
A touching film or piece of music can move me to tears.
When stakes are high, I check and re-check — sent messages, locked doors, submitted work.
